steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The basking shark runs 1.0–1.5 °C above ambient (heat retention), yet its temperature still declines as ambient temperature falls. Despite generating metabolic heat, its body temperature tracks the environment.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — yellowfin tuna and the shortfin mako shark are likely to be more variable than the body temperature of the basking shark.: The passage gives no evidence that yellowfin tuna and shortfin mako shark body temperatures are more variable than the basking shark's. ✗
  • Choice B — Pacific halibut and the basking shark are at least partly determined by the ambient water temperature.: Pacific halibut is fully ectothermic; the basking shark's temperature still tracks ambient despite heat retention. Both fit. ✓
  • Choice C — basking shark and the shortfin mako shark remain fixed even as ambient water temperature changes, while the body temperature of the Pacific halibut changes with variations in the ambient water temperature.: The passage says the basking shark's body temperature declines as ambient temperature falls, so it does not remain fixed. ✗
  • Choice D — basking shark and the yellowfin tuna remain fixed even if the ambient water temperature changes, while the body temperature of the oceanic whitetip shark varies even if the ambient temperature is stable.: No data support fixed body temperatures for basking sharks or yellowfin tuna, and the passage describes oceanic whitetip sharks as ectotherms whose body temperatures would track ambient conditions. ✗
That matches Choice B — Pacific halibut and the basking shark are at least partly determined by the ambient water temperature..
